# Passage: 8  
From a sociological perspective, religion is understood as a unified system of beliefs and practices that typically involves a broader community of believers who share common definitions of the sacred and the profane. Participation in religious organizations generally accomplishes something in the lives of participants, from providing a sense of meaning to establishing membership in a social network. By examining trends in religious participation, social scientists can develop a more holistic understanding about the role of religion as a social institution in modern society.  
Research has found that American society is less religious than it has been historically. For example, almost 30% of Americans raised within an actively religious household abandoned their faith when they became adults. Some social scientists have argued that this indicates increasing secularization in the United States. A survey of American adults over a 40-year period, from 1973 to 2013, asked respondents to indicate the level of confidence they had in organized religious structures. Respondents were able to choose from response options indicating a great deal, quite a lot, some, very little, or no confidence. The results of the survey are shown in Table 1.  
Table 1Americans’ Confidence in Organized Religion (1973–2013)
YearA great dealQuite a lotSomeVery littleNone
2013252332172
2003272330172
1993292429143
198339232691
197343222174
Note: Numbers are expressed as percentages of Americans reporting each response.
Table 2 illustrates the findings of the Pew Research Center’s 2007 Religious Landscape Survey on religious attendance at services across age groups. Researchers are able to determine if there are differences in religious attendance across various age demographics in a given society.  
Table 2Religious Attendance by Age (2007)

Adapted from “Confidence in Institutions.” ©2013 Gallup; “Religion among the Millennials.” ©2010 Pew Research Center.
